planting.note: Plant rhizome cuttings ('rhizomes') horizontally 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; hops climbs vigorously (up to 20 feet in a single season) — provide a very strong trellis or support. Only female plants produce usable cones.

directions: Plant rhizomes horizontally in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; hops is the perennial vine whose dried female flower cones ('hops') are the essential bittering and preserving agent in virtually all beer styles. Native to temperate North America and Europe, it is a vigorous, tendriled vine that dies to the ground each fall and re-emerges rapidly each spring to climb 15–20 feet on strings, trellises, or arbors. The soft green hop cones (strobiles) ripen in late summer; harvest when papery and fragrant, then dry immediately for use in home brewing. The young spring shoots emerging from the ground are edible as a vegetable, cooked like asparagus. The dried seed cones have traditional medicinal use as a mild sedative. Ornamental cultivars like 'Aureus' (golden-yellow foliage) are widely grown for their dramatic summer screening effect. Performs best in cool-summer climates (zones 3–7); struggles in the persistent heat of zone 8+.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; bottlebrush grass self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions and naturalizes to form a loose colony in moist woodland edge settings.

directions: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade — bottlebrush grass is one of the few native ornamental grasses that genuinely thrives in partial to light shade, making it exceptional for the shaded garden or woodland edge where most grasses cannot grow. The distinctive, upright, cylindrical seed heads — radiating individual spikelets outward from the stem in a perfect bottle-brush pattern — are among the most ornamental of any native grass, remaining attractive from summer through winter as they dry to tan. It grows naturally in moist, rich woodland edges, ravine banks, and moist forest floors throughout the eastern US. Self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions to naturalize. Outstanding for adding native grass texture to shaded areas of the garden or in combination with native wildflowers and ferns along a woodland edge.

planting.note: Plant root divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun to partial shade; American licorice spreads by deep, extensive root systems and can be difficult to remove once established — site it where expansion is acceptable.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun to partial shade; American licorice is the native North American relative of the commercial licorice plant (Glycyrrhiza glabra), native to the Great Plains and western prairies where it grows along stream banks, roadsides, and moist prairie edges. The thick, deep roots have a genuine licorice flavor from glycyrrhizin compounds (the same compound as commercial licorice) and were used extensively by Native American nations as a food and medicine — chewed fresh, dried as candy, brewed as tea, and used to treat sore throats, toothaches, and stomach complaints. The pinnate compound leaves and small white to pale violet flowers are attractive; the bur-like bristly seed pods cling to fur and clothing. A nitrogen-fixing prairie legume, it improves soil fertility. Spreads by rhizome; contain in smaller gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; white prairie clover has a deep taproot and does not transplant well once established — direct seeding in fall or early spring produces the best results.

directions: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; white prairie clover is one of the most valuable native prairie legumes, distinctive for its unique cylindrical flower spikes — the tiny white flowers open progressively upward from the base in a narrow band that travels up the spike over 2–3 weeks, creating an unusual effect quite different from any other native wildflower. The flowers are exceptionally attractive to native bees including specialist Dalea bees (Hesperapis and Protandrena species) that collect pollen exclusively from Dalea plants. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume that enriches the soil and is drought-tolerant and long-lived once established. The deep taproot provides winter hardiness and drought resistance. An outstanding native plant for dry, sunny prairie and meadow gardens, and one of the best plants for specialist native bee support.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; European wild ginger is slower to establish than native Asarum canadense but has glossier, more ornamental leaves and is more tolerant of hot, dry shade.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — European wild ginger is a low, slowly spreading shade groundcover with extraordinarily glossy, deep green, kidney-shaped leaves that remain evergreen through winter in zones 5–8, creating a lustrous, polished carpet under trees and shrubs. The leaves are significantly glossier and more ornamental than native wild ginger (A. canadense), which is deciduous. Small, brownish-purple, three-lobed flowers appear hidden at ground level in spring. It spreads slowly and politely by rhizome, creating a refined, weed-suppressing mat in appropriate conditions. It tolerates dry shade better than the native species once established. Unlike native Asarum, the European species does not host certain specialist insects, but its ornamental value is superior. An outstanding foliage plant for shaded woodland gardens in zones 4–8.
